tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Kinematics describes motion without considering forces. Start by identifying what kind of motion this is: is the object moving in one dimension or two? What quantities are given (position, velocity, acceleration, time) and what are you asked to find?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Use the kinematic relationships: v = v₀ + at, x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at², v² = v₀² + 2a(x − x₀). For projectile motion, treat horizontal and vertical motion independently — horizontal velocity stays constant (if air resistance is neglected), while vertical motion has constant acceleration g downward.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Draw a diagram with a coordinate system. Label initial conditions (x₀, v₀, a) and the unknown. Choose the kinematic equation that connects your knowns to your unknown without introducing extra variables. Watch sign conventions: pick a positive direction and stick to it.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: List your known values with units. Substitute into the chosen equation. If you have two unknowns, write a second equation (often from a different phase of motion or a component). Solve algebraically before plugging in numbers. Check that your answer has correct units and reasonable magnitude.

tier5.title: Full Solution Placeholder
tier5.content: Tier 5 is generated by the AI tutor with a complete solution and reasoning comparison.

tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Projectile motion is two-dimensional motion under gravity alone. The key insight: horizontal and vertical motions are independent. What launches the object, and at what angle? What point in the trajectory are you analyzing?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Decompose initial velocity: v₀ₓ = v₀ cos θ, v₀ᵧ = v₀ sin θ. Horizontal: aₓ = 0, so x = v₀ₓ t. Vertical: aᵧ = −g, so y = v₀ᵧ t − ½gt². At the peak, vᵧ = 0. Range and max height formulas come from these component equations.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Set up separate tables for x and y components. Time is the linking variable — find t from one direction, then use it in the other. For max height, use vᵧ = 0. For range, set y = 0 (launch and landing at same height) or use the given landing height.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Write v₀ₓ and v₀ᵧ explicitly. Solve for time to peak: t_peak = v₀ᵧ/g. Max height: y_max = v₀ᵧ²/(2g). Total flight time (same launch/land height): t_total = 2v₀ᵧ/g. Range: R = v₀ₓ × t_total. Substitute your numbers and verify units.

tier5.title: Full Solution Placeholder
tier5.content: Tier 5 is generated by the AI tutor with a complete solution and reasoning comparison.

tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Motion graphs relate position, velocity, or acceleration to time. What does the slope of each graph represent? What does the area under the curve represent?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: On a position-time graph: slope = velocity. On a velocity-time graph: slope = acceleration, area under curve = displacement. On an acceleration-time graph: area under curve = change in velocity. Constant slope means constant rate of change.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Identify which graph type you have. Read the question carefully — are you finding slope, area, or a specific value at a time? Break curved sections into triangles/trapezoids if estimating area, or use calculus relationships if given equations.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Mark the time interval of interest on the graph. Calculate slope using rise/run between two points, or area using geometric shapes. For piecewise graphs, sum contributions from each segment. Check sign: area below the axis counts as negative displacement.
